APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it proposes a novel decoder architecture for neural machine translation (chunk-level + word-level decoders) built on attention-based NMT (Sec.2/3) and evaluated on a standard WAT ’16 ASPEC English–Japanese task. The audience is core NMT/NLP researchers, the problem formulation (hierarchical decoding to model intra-/inter-chunk dependencies) is appropriate, and the methods and evaluation use community standards (GRU/attention, beam search, BLEU and RIBES, comparisons to tree-to-seq and other baselines). The paper is positioned with relevant prior work (Bahdanau et al., Eriguchi et al., Luong & Manning) and thus fits a top NLP/ML venue. | B(5): Yes. The paper proposes a decoder architecture for neural machine translation (chunk-level + word-level) addressing core NMT modelling questions (§3.1–3.3) and compares to relevant prior work (tree-to-seq, hierarchical RNNs, SMT chunking). It uses standard methods and evaluations expected at top venues (attention-based GRU models, ASPEC WAT ’16 English–Japanese data, BLEU and RIBES; §4, Results) and is clearly aimed at the NMT/NLP research community. The problem formulation, baselines, and positioning within related work (Eriguchi et al., Luong & Manning, Bahdanau et al.) make it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it proposes a novel decoder architecture for neural machine translation (chunk-level + word-level decoders) built on attention-based NMT (Sec.2/3) and evaluated on a standard WAT ’16 ASPEC English–Japanese task. The audience is core NMT/NLP researchers, the problem formulation (hierarchical decoding to model intra-/inter-chunk dependencies) is appropriate, and the methods and evaluation use community standards (GRU/attention, beam search, BLEU and RIBES, comparisons to tree-to-seq and other baselines). The paper is positioned with relevant prior work (Bahdanau et al., Eriguchi et al., Luong & Manning) and thus fits a top NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes. The paper proposes a decoder architecture for neural machine translation (chunk-level + word-level) addressing core NMT modelling questions (§3.1–3.3) and compares to relevant prior work (tree-to-seq, hierarchical RNNs, SMT chunking). It uses standard methods and evaluations expected at top venues (attention-based GRU models, ASPEC WAT ’16 English–Japanese data, BLEU and RIBES; §4, Results) and is clearly aimed at the NMT/NLP research community. The problem formulation, baselines, and positioning within related work (Eriguchi et al., Luong & Manning, Bahdanau et al.) make it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—capturing chunk/phrase structure and long-distance dependencies in NMT—by introducing a hierarchical chunk-level and word-level decoder (§3.1, Eqs.16–20) and two extensions (inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback, §§3.2–3.3). It reports concrete empirical gains (e.g., “our best model outperforms the best single NMT model reported in WAT ’16” and BLEU improvements vs. tree-to-seq and char-based baselines), showing the idea can improve real translation performance on English–Japanese ASPEC. While this approach opens clear follow-ups (combining with Tree‑LSTM/CNN encoders and further hierarchical decoding work) and is likely to be adopted for languages where reliable chunking exists (the paper leverages bunsetsu chunking with >99% accuracy), the evaluation is somewhat narrow and language-dependent, so the contribution is of moderate rather than transformative impact. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—modeling target-side (inter- vs intra-chunk) dependencies in NMT—by introducing a chunk-level decoder to capture global dependencies and a word-level decoder for intra-chunk word order (§3.1–3.3). Empirical results report meaningful gains (outperforming the best single NMT in WAT ’16 on ASPEC English–Japanese and improvements over tree-/character-based baselines), demonstrating practical impact. The approach opens clear follow-on directions (combining with Tree-LSTM/CNN encoders, hierarchical decoders) and has applicability beyond this language pair (and to other hierarchical generation tasks), though adoption may be moderated by reliance on chunking and task-specific considerations.

It reports concrete empirical gains (e.g., “our best model outperforms the best single NMT model reported in WAT ’16” and BLEU improvements vs. tree-to-seq and char-based baselines), showing the idea can improve real translation performance on English–Japanese ASPEC. While this approach opens clear follow-ups (combining with Tree‑LSTM/CNN encoders and further hierarchical decoding work) and is likely to be adopted for languages where reliable chunking exists (the paper leverages bunsetsu chunking with >99% accuracy), the evaluation is somewhat narrow and language-dependent, so the contribution is of moderate rather than transformative impact.

Empirical results report meaningful gains (outperforming the best single NMT in WAT ’16 on ASPEC English–Japanese and improvements over tree-/character-based baselines), demonstrating practical impact. The approach opens clear follow-on directions (combining with Tree-LSTM/CNN encoders, hierarchical decoders) and has applicability beyond this language pair (and to other hierarchical generation tasks), though adoption may be moderated by reliance on chunking and task-specific considerations.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (word-based seq2seq (Li et al.,2016), tree-to-seq and char-tree-to-seq (Eriguchi et al.,2016a/b)) using the WAT’16 ASPEC data and standard metrics (BLEU, RIBES), and reports wins over the best single NMT at WAT’16. It cites and discusses relevant prior chunk/S M T and hierarchical works (Koehn, Watanabe, Kim, Luong & Manning, etc.) and presents incremental model variants (Models 1–3), which aids analysis. However, some comparisons appear to rely on reported numbers with differing model/vocab setups (table shows different RNN types and |V|), and there is limited discussion ensuring exact parity of hyperparameters/conditions, so fairness/complete analysis is not fully established. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate recent baselines (word-based seq2seq (Li et al., 2016), tree-to-seq and character-based tree-to-seq (Eriguchi et al., 2016a/b)) and reports results on the WAT ’16 ASPEC dataset using BLEU and RIBES following the WAT ’16 evaluation procedure (see §4 and the result table). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the paper reports numeric gains and states it outperforms the best single NMT at WAT ’16. However, the paper provides only limited deeper analysis of when prior methods perform comparably or better and, beyond variant-model discussion (Models 1–3), lacks extensive ablation/diagnostic analysis or inclusion of some other strong contemporaneous baselines, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (word-based seq2seq (Li et al.,2016), tree-to-seq and char-tree-to-seq (Eriguchi et al.,2016a/b)) using the WAT’16 ASPEC data and standard metrics (BLEU, RIBES), and reports wins over the best single NMT at WAT’16. It cites and discusses relevant prior chunk/S M T and hierarchical works (Koehn, Watanabe, Kim, Luong & Manning, etc.) and presents incremental model variants (Models 1–3), which aids analysis. However, some comparisons appear to rely on reported numbers with differing model/vocab setups (table shows different RNN types and |V|), and there is limited discussion ensuring exact parity of hyperparameters/conditions, so fairness/complete analysis is not fully established.

Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the paper reports numeric gains and states it outperforms the best single NMT at WAT ’16. However, the paper provides only limited deeper analysis of when prior methods perform comparably or better and, beyond variant-model discussion (Models 1–3), lacks extensive ablation/diagnostic analysis or inclusion of some other strong contemporaneous baselines, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments compare against strong baselines on a standard ASPEC English–Japanese set (2M training pairs) and report BLEU/RIBES, including comparisons to word-based seq2seq and tree-to-seq models; the three presented variants (Model 1–3) serve as an explicit ablation of added components (inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback). Related work is discussed broadly and fairly (SMT/EBMT, tree encoders, hierarchical RNNs). Missing, however, are explicit discussions of failure cases or limitations and some reproduction details (exact hyperparameters, training epochs/model sizes) are not provided despite mentioning ADADELTA, beam search, vocab sizes and chunking tools. | B(3): The paper presents concrete experiments on ASPEC (WAT’16) with BLEU/RIBES evaluation and comparisons to reasonable baselines (word-based seq2seq, tree-to-seq, character-based) and reports improvements (see §4 Results and reported table), showing the three model variants (§3.1–3.3). However, there are no thorough ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g. effect of chunking errors, chunk size, or hyperparameter sweeps), limitations or failure cases are not discussed, and reproducibility is incomplete (missing exact hyperparameters, training/validation splits, and many training details despite noting ADADELTA, beam search, vocab sizes). Related work coverage is fairly comprehensive with multiple citations to hierarchical/tree models and SMT/EBMT history.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments compare against strong baselines on a standard ASPEC English–Japanese set (2M training pairs) and report BLEU/RIBES, including comparisons to word-based seq2seq and tree-to-seq models; the three presented variants (Model 1–3) serve as an explicit ablation of added components (inter-chunk connections and word-to-chunk feedback). Related work is discussed broadly and fairly (SMT/EBMT, tree encoders, hierarchical RNNs). Missing, however, are explicit discussions of failure cases or limitations and some reproduction details (exact hyperparameters, training epochs/model sizes) are not provided despite mentioning ADADELTA, beam search, vocab sizes and chunking tools.

However, there are no thorough ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g. effect of chunking errors, chunk size, or hyperparameter sweeps), limitations or failure cases are not discussed, and reproducibility is incomplete (missing exact hyperparameters, training/validation splits, and many training details despite noting ADADELTA, beam search, vocab sizes). Related work coverage is fairly comprehensive with multiple citations to hierarchical/tree models and SMT/EBMT history.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper introduces a chunk-based decoder architecture comprising a chunk-level decoder and a word-level decoder (§3.1.2–3.1.3) and concrete variants (Model 2 inter-chunk connections; Model 3 word-to-chunk feedback). This applies and combines established ideas—hierarchical RNNs and chunk/phrase units from SMT and prior hierarchical seq2seq work (Watanabe et al. 2003; Li et al. 2015; Luong & Manning 2016)—to the decoder side, which the authors claim was previously unaddressed. The specific mechanisms are useful and non-trivial extensions, but the overall approach is an incremental adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. | B(3): The paper proposes a chunk-based decoder (a chunk-level decoder plus a word-level decoder) and three variants (standard, inter-chunk connection, word-to-chunk feedback) to model intra-chunk and inter-chunk dependencies (§3.1–3.3). While framing chunk-based decoding for NMT appears new relative to prior NMT decoders (“chunk structure ... has not been considered in the decoders of neural machine translation”), the idea builds on existing notions (chunk/phrase SMT, hierarchical RNNs, tree-to-sequence encoders cited in the paper) and thus reads as a non-trivial but incremental architectural extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a chunk-based decoder architecture comprising a chunk-level decoder and a word-level decoder (§3.1.2–3.1.3) and concrete variants (Model 2 inter-chunk connections; Model 3 word-to-chunk feedback). This applies and combines established ideas—hierarchical RNNs and chunk/phrase units from SMT and prior hierarchical seq2seq work (Watanabe et al. 2003; Li et al. 2015; Luong & Manning 2016)—to the decoder side, which the authors claim was previously unaddressed. The specific mechanisms are useful and non-trivial extensions, but the overall approach is an incremental adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

While framing chunk-based decoding for NMT appears new relative to prior NMT decoders (“chunk structure ... has not been considered in the decoders of neural machine translation”), the idea builds on existing notions (chunk/phrase SMT, hierarchical RNNs, tree-to-sequence encoders cited in the paper) and thus reads as a non-trivial but incremental architectural extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(3): The paper is generally organized into preliminaries, model descriptions, and experiments, but the flow is disrupted by many duplicated paragraphs and stray TeX/fragments (e.g. repeated introductory paragraphs and citation blocks) which makes reading choppy. The main idea (chunk-level + word-level decoders) is stated, but contributions are scattered rather than clearly highlighted up front. Notation is mostly defined (chunk/word states s(c), s(w)) yet contains inconsistencies/typos (the attention softmax in Eq. (13) sums over the wrong index and references to missing Eqs. (7),(8),(15)), and figures/tables are referenced but not effectively presented (Figure 2/3/4 missing, result table fragment is poorly formatted). Overall writing is understandable but marred by repetition, formatting errors, and occasional ambiguity, so some careful editing is needed. | B(2): The paper has an overall logical structure (intro → models → experiments) but presentation is marred by heavy repetition and formatting errors (e.g. the paragraph ‘‘Neural machine translation (NMT) performs...’’ appears many times verbatim), which breaks readability. Main contributions are mentioned (chunk-based decoders) but not highlighted cleanly early as a concise list. Notation is inconsistently presented and sometimes unclear or referentially broken (references to Eq. (15) that is not present in the excerpt; ambiguous symbols like s(w)Jk−1 and s̃(c)k used without consistent indexing explanation). Figures/tables are frequently referenced (Figure 3/4, result table) but are not shown or are poorly formatted in the text, and several sentences contain duplicated fragments, indicating the need for careful copyediting and restructuring.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents clear model architectures (Models 1–3) and reports empirical improvements on ASPEC/WAT16 baselines, but key technical details are missing or inconsistent. Eq. (13) for attention is written incorrectly (denominator sums over j′ rather than source positions i), indicating a mathematical error in the writeup, and the provided excerpt does not explain how chunk boundaries/lengths are predicted or trained (how chunks are derived at training vs. inference), which is central to the method. The experiments compare reasonable baselines (word- and tree-based NMT) on a standard corpus, but no statistical significance tests or full experimental details are given in the excerpt. Given these issues (missing methodological detail and a noted equation error) the claims are plausible but not fully substantiated by the provided text. | B(3): The paper clearly states the main claim (chunk-based decoders improve NMT) and provides experimental results (BLEU/RIBES on ASPEC Eng-Jpn, comparisons to word-based and tree-to-seq baselines) that support the claim, and the mathematical model (Eqs. 11–22) is standard and appears correct. However, experimental methodology has gaps: vocabulary sizes and RNN types differ across compared systems, there is no mention of statistical significance or detailed hyperparameter/epoch/parameter-count controls, and the choice to truncate to the first 2M sentence pairs could bias results. There is also an important unstated/unclear component in the provided text — how chunk boundaries are predicted or learned and how chunk segmentation is integrated into training — which is an unsupported assumption affecting generality beyond Japanese. Given these concerns, the claims are only partially but not fully validated by the evidence shown.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear model architectures (Models 1–3) and reports empirical improvements on ASPEC/WAT16 baselines, but key technical details are missing or inconsistent. Eq. The experiments compare reasonable baselines (word- and tree-based NMT) on a standard corpus, but no statistical significance tests or full experimental details are given in the excerpt. Given these issues (missing methodological detail and a noted equation error) the claims are plausible but not fully substantiated by the provided text.

However, experimental methodology has gaps: vocabulary sizes and RNN types differ across compared systems, there is no mention of statistical significance or detailed hyperparameter/epoch/parameter-count controls, and the choice to truncate to the first 2M sentence pairs could bias results. There is also an important unstated/unclear component in the provided text — how chunk boundaries are predicted or learned and how chunk segmentation is integrated into training — which is an unsupported assumption affecting generality beyond Japanese. Given these concerns, the claims are only partially but not fully validated by the evidence shown.
